core_intuitions: The from-scratch implementations form a bottom-up dependency arc: math primitives (autograd) are the root, everything downstream is a specialization of 'forward builds a graph, backward traverses it'.

One shared stack carries two distinct flows: the forward/inference path (text -> tokens -> embeddings -> blocks -> logits -> sampled token) and the training/gradient path (loss -> autograd -> LoRA-restricted updates -> alignment objectives).
Components are swappable modules with fixed interfaces: BPE<->unigram, sinusoidal<->RoPE, dense attention<->FlashAttention, so the same skeleton supports many concrete choices.

The book's learning arc is organized in tiers by role (foundations -> transformer -> post-training -> hardware kernels -> inference-time algorithms); reading them in order is a complete construction of an LLM system.
Post-training sits ON TOP of a frozen pretrained model: LoRA restricts what can change, DPO/GRPO change the objective, but none re-run pretraining.

existing.name: fsindex-stack-dataflow
existing.issue: Content and structure are strong and it correctly conveys the forward spine + training branch + FlashAttention swap. But in dark theme the section headers ('FORWARD SPINE', 'TRAINING BRANCH') and the small inline flow labels ('token IDs', '(B, T, d_model)', 'next token', 'gradients', 'cross-entropy loss') are low-contrast and nearly disappear; the node boxes are hardcoded white fills rather than theme-aware surfaces, which reads as jarring against the dark canvas.
existing.improvement: Make all text theme-aware via currentColor / CSS variables so headers and edge labels keep strong contrast in both themes; convert the node fills to a theme-aware surface token (light surface in light mode, dark elevated surface with light text in dark mode) instead of hardcoded white. Keep the exact layout, arrows, section numbering (§ tags), the FlashAttention swap callout, and the legend unchanged — this is purely a contrast/theming pass, not a redesign.

new_figures.name: fsindex-learning-arc-tiers
new_figures.concept: The whole book's from-scratch code as five stacked tiers (foundations, transformer, post-training, kernels, inference), each tier a shelf of named implementations, with swappable-slot markers showing interchangeable pieces.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter's opening explicitly frames the implementations as a 'deliberate learning arc' and 'the dependency graph of the entire textbook's code', but the only existing figure is a runtime dataflow (a vertical pipe), which does not surface the pedagogical build-order tiers or the swap-slot idea except as one FlashAttention callout. For an INDEX chapter, the tiered build-order-plus-swappability view is the foundational mental model that lets a reader locate any implementation and understand what it rests on — a genuinely distinct framing from the dataflow figure, not a re-teach of any single mechanism.
new_figures.placement: Section '12. How the Implementations Fit Together' (near the Dependency Graph Summary table), as a visual companion to that table.
new_figures.brief: A static infographic of five horizontal tiers stacked vertically, bottom = foundation, top = user-facing, with an upward 'builds on' arrow along the left margin spanning all tiers to signal the bottom-up arc. Tier labels (small caps, theme-aware text) bottom-to-top: (1) MATHEMATICAL FOUNDATIONS, (2) THE TRANSFORMER, (3) POST-TRAINING & ALIGNMENT, (4) HARDWARE KERNELS, (5) INFERENCE-TIME ALGORITHMS. Inside each tier place labeled cards (rounded rectangles, theme-aware surface + text, small illustrative only — no invented numbers): Tier 1 => 'Scalar Autograd (Value)'; Tier 2 => 'BPE Tokenizer', 'Embeddings + RoPE', 'Attention', 'GPT (assembles all)'; Tier 3 => 'LoRA', 'DPO', 'GRPO'; Tier 4 => 'FlashAttention (online softmax)'; Tier 5 => 'Samplers', 'Speculative Decoding', 'RAG Pipeline'. Show TWO kinds of relationship marks, distinguished in a small legend: (a) thin solid 'builds on' arrows crossing tier boundaries for the key dependencies — Autograd -> every tier above (draw one representative arrow, or a faint 'rests on autograd' band), Attention -> GPT, GPT -> LoRA, LoRA/SFT -> DPO & GRPO, GPT logits -> Samplers; (b) a distinct dashed double-headed 'swappable slot' connector on the interchangeable pairs: BPE <-> (unigram) as a ghosted alternative card, Attention <-> FlashAttention (drawn as FlashAttention in tier 4 pointing to the Attention card in tier 2 with a 'drop-in replaces' dashed link), sinusoidal <-> RoPE inside the Embeddings card. Keep the reading order unambiguous: tiers read bottom-to-top for build order, cards left-to-right within a tier for pipeline order. Everything must fit inside the viewBox with no overlapping labels; the swap connectors must not tangle with the build-on arrows (route them on opposite sides or use clearly different dash/weight). Fully legible as a single static frame in both light and dark themes; use no hardcoded primary colors — rely on theme variables / currentColor and a single accent for the 'swappable' marks only. A compact legend distinguishes: solid arrow = builds on, dashed double-arrow = swappable slot.

summary: This appendix is a synthesis/index of every from-scratch implementation in the book; each underlying mechanism is taught (and illustrated) in its own home chapter, so this chapter's figures should convey only connective tissue: the dependency arc, the forward-vs-gradient dual flow, and swappability. The single existing figure (fsindex-stack-dataflow) already conveys the runtime dataflow well and should be KEPT structurally but REFINED for dark-theme legibility (invisible section headers and low-contrast edge labels, plus hardcoded white node fills). One NEW static figure is warranted: a five-tier 'learning arc' that visualizes the book's explicit build-order plus the swappable-slot idea — a distinct, foundational mental model for an index chapter that the dataflow figure does not provide. No animation is needed here; every intuition in this chapter is structural and reads from a single static frame.
